lesson:
必 (certainly, necessarily) + 要 (need, essential point). What is certainly needed. パスポートが必要です (a passport is necessary). 必要な書類 (necessary documents). It works as a な-adjective (必要な) and as a noun (必要がある — there is a need).

The kanji 必 is worth examining. It looks like a heart (心) pierced by a line — and some etymologists read it that way: something that pierces straight to the heart, unavoidable, certain. Others see it as a stake driven into the ground — something fixed, immovable. Either way, it carries absolute certainty. 必ず (かならず, without fail) uses the same kanji.

Opposite: 不必要 (ふひつよう, unnecessary) — but in conversation people more often say 必要ない or いらない (don't need it).

Dictionaryひつよう
Negativeひつようじゃない
Pastひつようだった
Past negひつようじゃなかった
Adverbひつように
て-formひつようで

This is not 持つ (to hold, possess) — though they sound identical. This もつ means to last, to endure, to hold up over time. この電池はよくもつ (this battery lasts well). このパンは三日はもつ (this bread will keep for three days). 体がもたない (my body can't take it anymore).

It's usually written in hiragana precisely to avoid confusion with 持つ. When you see もつ in hiragana followed by a duration or without a direct object, it's this word — the durability one.

The negative もたない is especially common and expressive. あと十分ももたない (it won't even last ten more minutes). It conveys that something is at its limit — fuel running out, patience wearing thin, food going bad.

Particles: もつ takes が — the thing that lasts is the subject. 電池がもつ (the battery lasts). No direct object.

Passive: Rare — you wouldn't naturally put durability into passive voice.

Causative を／にもたせる: to make something last. 冷蔵庫に入れてもたせる (put it in the fridge to make it last).

Politeもちますが
Polite negもちませんが
Pastもったが
Polite pastもちましたが
て-formもってが

You already know 開ける (あける) — to open something (transitive). 開く read as あく is the intransitive partner: the thing opens by itself. ドアが開いた (the door opened). 店が開いている (the store is open).

The reading trap: 開く has two intransitive readings. As あく, it's everyday opening — doors, stores, eyes, mouths. As ひらく, it's more abstract or ceremonial — flowers blooming, events being held, possibilities opening up. ドアがあく (the door opens). 花がひらく (a flower opens/blooms). Context and furigana are your only guides in written text.

The pair 開く/閉まる (open/close, both intransitive) matches the transitive pair 開ける/閉める. Lock these four together in your mind and door-related Japanese becomes effortless.

Particles: 開く (あく) takes が — the thing that opens is the subject. 窓が開いている (the window is open).

Passive: Rare for intransitive verbs. Use the plain form.

Causative を／に開かせる: making something open. Usually you'd just use 開ける instead.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 開ける (あける)

昨年 and 去年 (きょねん) both mean "last year," but they live in different registers. 去年 is everyday conversation — what you'd say to a friend. 昨年 is what you'd write in a business email, hear in a news broadcast, or read in a formal report. Same meaning, different clothing.

昨 means "yesterday/previous" and shows up in a family of formal time words: 昨日 (さくじつ, yesterday — formal; the casual reading is きのう), 昨夜 (さくや, last night — formal), 昨年 (さくねん, last year). The さく reading always signals formality.

When in doubt, use 去年 in conversation and 昨年 in writing. Mixing them up won't cause a misunderstanding, but using 昨年 in casual chat sounds stiff, like saying "the preceding calendar year" when you just mean "last year."

meaning: to get healed, be cured, get well
lesson:
You know 直る (なおる) — to get fixed, repaired (machines, objects). 治る is the same sound, same conjugation, but for the body: to heal, to recover from illness. 風邪が治った (my cold got better). 傷が治る (the wound heals).

The kanji 治 has the water radical (氵) plus 台 — originally the character depicted controlling or managing water, as in irrigation and flood control. From "controlling water" it drifted to "governing" (政治, せいじ, politics) and then to "curing" — restoring order to a disordered body. The metaphor: disease is chaos in the body, and healing is bringing it under control.

Both 治る and 直る are intransitive — the healing or fixing happens on its own. Their transitive partners: 治す (なおす, to cure) and 直す (なおす, to fix).

Particles: 治る takes が — the illness or injury is the subject. 病気が治る (the illness gets better).

Passive: Rare — illness healing is already a spontaneous process.

Causative を／に治らせる: making something heal. Uncommon — you'd use 治す instead.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 治す (なおす)

Negativeなおらないが
Politeなおりますが
Polite negなおりませんが
Pastなおったが
Polite pastなおりましたが
て-formなおってが
Volitionalなおろうが
Polite volなおりましょうが

Causativeなおらせるを／に
Caus-passなおらせられるに

Imperativeなおれが

In Japan, prices are in 円. But the moment a conversation turns to foreign goods, travel budgets, or exchange rates, ドル enters. 今、一ドル何円？ (what's the dollar at now?) is a question any internationally-minded Japanese person might ask daily.

There's a cultural layer here. For decades, the ドル/円 exchange rate has been a bellwether of Japan's economic health, making front-page news. 円高 (えんだか, strong yen) and 円安 (えんやす, weak yen) are defined against the dollar. When Japanese news says ドルが上がった (the dollar went up), everyone understands the economic implications.

Walk into a Japanese office and someone will eventually sigh, システムが落ちた (the system crashed). In business Japanese, システム almost always means IT — the network, the software, the infrastructure. This is where the katakana word lives: technology and modernity.

Japanese has its own words for "system" — 制度 (せいど, institutional system — tax system, education system), 仕組み (しくみ, mechanism — how something works), 体制 (たいせい, regime/structure). But none of these cover what happens when you plug in a computer. システム filled that gap and now dominates: システムエンジニア (systems engineer), システム開発 (system development), システム障害 (system failure). If you work in tech in Japan, this word will follow you home.

meaning: more than or equal to, that's all
lesson:
以上 has two very different uses that only context distinguishes.

Quantity: "X or more." 十人以上 (ten or more people). 三十度以上 (30 degrees or higher). Crucially, 以上 includes the number it attaches to. "Ten or more" means ten counts. This is different from English "more than ten" (which excludes ten). The distinction matters on exams and in legal contexts.

Conclusion: "That is all." 以上です at the end of a presentation or announcement means "that concludes my remarks." 報告は以上です (that's the end of the report). It's crisp, professional, final — the verbal equivalent of closing a folder.

The pair: 以上 (≥, above) and 以下 (いか, ≤, below). 十八歳以上 (18 and older). 十二歳以下 (12 and younger). Both include the boundary number.

最近どう？ (how've you been lately?) — this is how catch-up conversations start in Japanese. 最近 is the first word out of the gate, and it covers an elastic span of time. Yesterday? Last month? This whole season? All valid. The word has no fixed clock — it just means "the recent past that feels relevant right now."

最近、髪切った？ (did you get a haircut recently?) could refer to yesterday or two weeks ago. 最近の若者は (young people these days...) stretches "recently" to mean the entire current generation. The vagueness is a feature: 最近 lets you reference a period without committing to exactly how long ago it was.

Grammar-wise, it pulls double duty as noun and adverb with no particle needed. 最近読んだ本 (a book I recently read). 最近の天気 (the recent weather). The kanji — 最 (most) + 近 (near) — literally mean "the nearest." Nearest to now.

世界 started as a Buddhist term — it meant the entire universe of sentient beings, the bounded realm we all share. That origin still echoes in how the word is used. 世界 isn't just the physical globe; it's any complete, self-contained realm. 音楽の世界 (the world of music). 夢の世界 (the world of dreams). Each one has its own rules, its own inhabitants.

Two compounds you'll see constantly: 世界中 (せかいじゅう, all over the world) and 世界一 (せかいいち, the best in the world). That 〜中 pattern — adding じゅう to a place word to mean "throughout" — works everywhere: 日本中, 町中, 学校中. And 世界一 carries real weight — when Japanese media calls someone 世界一, it's a superlative that demands to be taken literally.

The full English word "computer," reshaped by Japanese phonology. Every consonant gets a vowel, every cluster gets broken apart: com-pyu-u-ta-a. The long ー marks are stretching the vowels to approximate the English rhythm.

In practice, almost nobody says コンピューター in casual conversation. They say パソコン (from "personal computer") for a desktop or laptop, or スマホ (from "smartphone") for a phone. コンピューター shows up more in technical, formal, or general contexts — コンピューターサイエンス (computer science), コンピューターウイルス (computer virus). It's the category word; パソコン is what people actually use at their desks.

There's a stylistic choice in how to spell it: コンピューター (with the final ー) vs. コンピュータ (without it). Technical writing sometimes drops the trailing ー; everyday usage keeps it. Both are correct.

This is the second time やる appears in the Core 1000 — word 172 was the first. That repetition tells you how common and how versatile this verb is. It means "to do" but with a hands-on, action-oriented edge that する doesn't have.

する is neutral: 勉強する (study). やる is punchy: やるしかない (there's nothing to do but do it). やってみる (give it a try). やった！ (I did it! / Yes!). Where する is a button press, やる is rolling up your sleeves.

The "give" meaning is separate and old-fashioned: 花に水をやる (water the flowers — give water to the flowers). 犬にえさをやる (feed the dog). This "giving" only goes to inferiors, animals, or plants — never to equals or superiors. For people, use あげる instead.

Particles: やる takes を for the thing you do: 仕事をやる. For the "give" meaning, に marks the recipient and を marks the thing given: 猫にえさをやる.

Potential がやれる: が — 俺にもやれる (even I can do it).

Passive にやられる: に marks the agent. 敵にやられた (I got beaten by the enemy). やられる often means being done in or defeated.

Causative に…をやらせる: に marks who you make do it. 後輩にやらせる (make the junior do it).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryやるを
Negativeやらないを
Politeやりますを
Polite negやりませんを
Pastやったを
Polite pastやりましたを
て-formやってを
Volitionalやろうを
Polite volやりましょうを
Passiveやられるに
Causativeやらせるに／を
Caus-passやらせられるに
Potentialやれるが
Imperativeやれを
Imp なさいやりなさいを
Cond ～ばやればを
Cond ～たらやったらを

どういう意味？ (what does that mean?) — you will say this sentence more than almost any other while learning Japanese. It's the universal "explain that to me" request. Memorize it first, understand its components later.

The kanji tell an interesting story: 意 (mind) + 味 (flavor). The flavor of a thought. Japanese treats meaning as something you taste — you roll a word around in your mind the way you'd roll food around in your mouth. The metaphor works in reverse too: 味がある (it has flavor) can mean something is meaningful or interesting.

意味がない goes beyond "meaningless" — it means "pointless, not worth doing." もう遅いから行っても意味がない (it's already late, so going would be pointless). When something has no 意味, it has no purpose, no value, no reason to exist. The word holds both definition and justification.

増える is intransitive — things increase on their own. Population grows, work piles up, weight creeps on. 仕事が増えた (work has increased). 体重が増えている (I've been gaining weight). The transitive partner is 増やす (ふやす) — to increase something deliberately.

The kanji 増 has the earth radical (土) and a component meaning "layers." Earth piling up in layers — things accumulating, growing. It's the kanji of gradual accumulation rather than sudden explosion.

増える pairs naturally with 減る (へる, to decrease). Learn them together: 人口が増える / 人口が減る. 給料が増える / 給料が減る. They're two sides of every graph.

Particles: 増える takes が — the thing increasing is the subject. 人口が増える (the population increases).

Passive: Already intransitive, so passive is unnatural. Use the plain form.

Causative を／に増えさせる: uncommon — use 増やす instead when you want to express making something increase.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 増やす (ふやす)

The kanji 選 has the movement radical (辶) and a complex right side that originally depicted two people being sorted — the act of selection, of separating the chosen from the rest. The same kanji appears in 選手 (せんしゅ, athlete — "selected player") and 選挙 (せんきょ, election — "selecting and raising up").

えらぶ covers everything from picking a restaurant to electing a president. 好きなのを選んで (pick whichever one you like). 言葉を選ぶ (choose one's words carefully). That second example is interesting — it implies deliberation, care, filtering. When you 選ぶ, you're considering options and making a judgment.

選ぶ vs 決める: 選ぶ is choosing from options. 決める (きめる) is deciding, committing. You 選ぶ first, then 決める. レストランを選んで、メニューを決める (choose a restaurant, then decide what to order).

Particles: 選ぶ takes を — the thing chosen is the direct object. プレゼントを選ぶ (choose a present). Use から for what you're choosing from: 三つの中から選ぶ (choose from three).

Potential が選べる: が — what you can choose becomes the subject. 色が選べる (you can choose the color).

Passive に選ばれる: に marks who does the choosing. キャプテンに選ばれた (I was chosen as captain).

Causative に…を選ばせる: に marks who you let choose. 子供に好きなおもちゃを選ばせる (let the kid choose their favorite toy).

Japanese has two words for "life" and using the wrong one sounds strange. 人生 (じんせい) is your whole life story — birth to death, the grand arc. 生活 is Tuesday. It's the daily grind: what you eat, how you commute, whether you can pay rent. 生活費 (せいかつひ, living expenses). 生活が苦しい (daily life is tough — financially). 生活のために働く (work to make a living).

生活する as a verb means "to live" with emphasis on the everyday experience: 日本で生活する (live in Japan). Compare with 住む, which just means to reside. You 住む at an address. You 生活する in a routine — groceries, trains, seasons, neighbors. The word holds the full texture of being somewhere, not just the coordinates.

進める is the transitive "advance" — you push something forward. A plan, a project, a conversation, a clock hand. 計画を進める (advance the plan). 話を進める (move the conversation forward). 時計を五分進める (set the clock five minutes ahead).

Its intransitive partner 進む (すすむ) means things advance on their own — work progresses, time moves forward, you walk ahead. 進める adds your hand on the wheel: you're the one making it happen.

進める vs 勧める: Both are read すすめる. 進める = move something forward. 勧める = recommend something. Completely different meanings, same sound. Written Japanese distinguishes them; spoken Japanese relies on context.

Particles: 進める takes を — the thing you advance is your direct object. プロジェクトを進める (advance the project).

Potential が進められる: が — 計画が進められる (the plan can be advanced).

Passive に進められる: に marks who advances it. 議長に話を進められた (the chairman moved the discussion forward).

Causative に…を進めさせる: に marks who you make advance something. 部下に作業を進めさせる (have the subordinate move the work forward).

続ける is the transitive "continue" — you keep doing something by choice. 勉強を続ける (continue studying). 走り続ける (keep running). Its intransitive partner 続く (つづく) means something continues on its own — a road extends, rain persists, a story goes on.

The most important pattern: verb stem + 続ける. Attach 続ける to the masu-stem of any verb and it means "keep doing that verb." 食べ続ける (keep eating), 話し続ける (keep talking), 信じ続ける (keep believing). This is one of the most productive compound-verb patterns in Japanese. Once you learn it, you can express sustained action with any verb in the language.

The kanji 続 has the thread radical (糸) — threads being drawn out continuously, one after another. An unbroken filament. That image of an unspooling thread is exactly what 続ける feels like: pulling the action forward without letting it snap.

Particles: 続ける takes を — the thing you continue is the direct object. 仕事を続ける (continue working).

Potential が続けられる: が — この生活が続けられない (I can't keep living like this).

Passive に続けられる: に marks the agent. Uncommon in practice.

Causative に…を続けさせる: に marks who you make continue. 生徒に練習を続けさせる (make students keep practicing).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 続く (つづく)

ほとんど flips meaning based on the sentence's polarity, and getting this wrong will say the opposite of what you mean.

With negative verbs: "hardly, barely, almost not." ほとんど寝ていない (I've hardly slept). ほとんど食べなかった (I barely ate). This is the more common usage.

With positive verbs: "almost, mostly, nearly all." 宿題はほとんど終わった (homework is almost done). ほとんどの人が賛成した (most people agreed).

The pattern is the same in both cases: ほとんど means "approaching the limit." In a positive sentence, it approaches completion. In a negative sentence, it approaches zero. The polarity of the verb determines which direction the approach goes.

No kanji in standard use — this is almost always written in hiragana. The kanji 殆ど exists but is uncommon outside formal writing.

会 (meeting, gathering) + 社 (shrine, company). People gathered at a shared place. 社 originally referred to the local earth god's shrine — the center of a community. Over time it came to mean any organized group, and eventually settled into its modern meaning of "company." A company is, at root, a group gathered around a common purpose.

In Japanese work culture, 会社 is more than your employer. It's an identity. 会社員 (かいしゃいん, company employee — literally "company member") is how millions of Japanese people answer the question "what do you do?" Not "I work in marketing" or "I'm an engineer" — just "I'm a company person." The company comes first, the role second. うちの会社 (our company — note the うち, the in-group marker) signals belonging.

会社を辞める (quit the company) is one of the weightiest decisions in Japanese professional life. 会社に行く isn't just "go to work" — it's "go to the company," the physical and social entity you belong to. The word carries the weight of loyalty, obligation, and group identity that shapes Japanese working life.

会 (meeting) + 社 (shrine, society). A place where people gather. But 社 is the interesting piece: its left radical is 示, the altar radical, and 土 on the right is earth. A sacred mound of earth — a shrine to the local land god. In ancient Japan, the 社 was where a community came together. That's why "company" and "shrine" share a kanji: both are places that bind people into a collective.

In Japan, 会社 isn't just where you work. It's closer to a social identity. 会社員 (かいしゃいん, company employee) is a standard answer to "what do you do?" — not your specific role, just the fact that you belong to a company. 会社を辞める (quit a company) carries weight that "I quit my job" doesn't in English. You're leaving the group.

会社 vs 企業: 企業 (きぎょう) also means company, but it's formal and refers to the business as an economic entity — the kind of word you'd see in financial news. 会社 is the word people actually use when talking about where they work. うちの会社 (my company — notice the うち, marking it as in-group).

You've already met this kanji as うち — the warm, tribal word for home, your in-group. いえ is the same character wearing different clothes. Where うち means "my place, my people," いえ means the physical structure. Walls, roof, rooms. 新しい家を建てる (build a new house). 家が大きい (the house is big). You can point at an いえ. You belong to an うち.

But いえ has a second life that うち doesn't: lineage. 家を継ぐ (inherit the family line). いい家の出 (from a good family). In this sense, いえ is the household stretching across generations — the family as an institution. This is the same kanji that produces 家族 (family), 家庭 (household), and 家 as a suffix meaning "-ist" or "expert": 作家 (さっか, writer), 画家 (がか, painter), 専門家 (せんもんか, specialist).

meaning: many, much, a lot, a large amount
lesson:
This is 多い in disguise. Remember how 多い can't go directly before a noun — you can't say 多い人? This is the workaround. 多く is the adverbial/noun form, and it behaves completely differently from the adjective it came from.

Two patterns to lock in. First, 多くの + noun: 多くの人 (many people), 多くの場合 (in many cases). This is the standard way to say "many [things]" that 多い can't do on its own. Second, 多く as a standalone noun meaning "the majority": 多くは学生だ (most of them are students).

The shift from い-adjective to く-form is productive across the language — 近い → 近く (nearby), 遠い → 遠く (far away) — but 多く is the one you'll use most, precisely because 多い has that grammatical hole it needs to fill.

話す (to speak) minus the verb ending gives you the noun: 話, the thing that was spoken. But where English needs three different words — conversation, story, talk — Japanese uses just this one. いい話 could be "a good story," "a nice conversation," or "good news" depending on context. 話がある (I have something to tell you) — and the tone of voice tells you whether it's exciting or ominous.

The kanji 話 is 言 (words, speech) plus 舌 (tongue). Words coming off the tongue. Straightforward, and that's fitting — 話 is one of those bedrock nouns that shows up constantly because Japanese conversation talks about talking all the time. 話を聞く (listen to what someone says). 話が長い (the story is long / they talk too much). 話にならない (it doesn't even count as a conversation — it's hopeless).

Heads up: 話 read as はなし is the noun. In compounds, the reading shifts to わ: 電話 (でんわ, telephone), 会話 (かいわ, conversation). Same kanji, two readings depending on whether it's standing alone or inside a compound.

上がる is the intransitive partner of 上げる (to raise something). Things 上がる on their own: prices rise, temperatures climb, rain stops, someone gets nervous. You 上げる things deliberately: you raise your hand, lift a box, turn up the volume. This pair — 上がる / 上げる — is one of the most common 自動詞/他動詞 splits in the language.

What makes 上がる interesting is how far it stretches beyond literal rising. 値段が上がる (prices go up) is predictable. 雨が上がる (the rain stops) is less obvious — the rain "rises" off the ground, clearing away. 緊張で上がる (get nervous / freeze up from tension) — your blood pressure rises, your composure floats away. 家に上がる (step up into a house) — Japanese houses have a raised floor, so entering is literally stepping up.

That last usage connects to a cultural rule: in Japan, you remove shoes at the 玄関 (entryway) before 上がる-ing into the house. The physical step up marks the boundary between outside and inside. どうぞお上がりください (please come in) is the polite invitation.

Particles: 上がる is intransitive — things rise on their own. The thing rising takes が: 温度が上がる. When entering a space (stepping up), it takes に: 家に上がる.

Passive に上がられる: indirect passive — someone's rising affects you. 値段に上がられると困る (if prices rise on me, I'm in trouble). に marks the thing whose rising impacts you.

Causative を／に上がらせる: making or letting something/someone rise. を leans coercive, に leans permissive. お客さんを部屋に上がらせる (have the guest come up into the room).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 上げる (あげる)
Dictionaryあがるが
Negativeあがらないが
Politeあがりますが
Polite negあがりませんが
Pastあがったが
Polite pastあがりましたが
て-formあがってが
Volitionalあがろうが
Polite volあがりましょうが
Passiveあがられるに
Causativeあがらせるを／に
Caus-passあがらせられるに
Potentialあがれるが
Imperativeあがれが
Imp なさいあがりなさいが
Cond ～ばあがればが
Cond ～たらあがったらが

meaning: now, soon; already (past), anymore (neg); another, again
lesson:
Three words wearing one disguise. もう has three distinct meanings, and context is the only way to tell them apart:

もう + past = "already." もう食べた (I already ate). The action happened sooner than expected. もう + negative = "not anymore." もう食べない (I won't eat anymore / I'm done eating). Something that was true has stopped. もう + quantity = "another, one more." もう一つ (one more). もう少し (a little more).

The thread connecting all three: もう always signals a shift from the current state. Something has already shifted (past). Something won't shift back (negative). Something needs to shift further (quantity). It's a word about thresholds being crossed.

もう vs まだ: These two form a natural pair. もう means the change happened. まだ means it hasn't. もう来た？ (did they come already?) / まだ来ていない (they haven't come yet). Keep them as a pair and they calibrate each other.

The kanji 集 is a bird (隹) landing on a tree (木). Not one bird — the idea is many birds converging on one tree. A flock gathering. That image is exactly what 集める means: you actively bring scattered things to one place.

集める is transitive — you do the collecting. Its intransitive partner is 集まる (あつまる) — things gather on their own. データを集める (collect data — you go get it). 人が集まる (people gather — they come by themselves). The める/まる pair is one of Japanese's most productive: the める version means you cause it, the まる version means it happens naturally.

What can you 集める? Almost anything. 切手を集める (collect stamps). 注目を集める (attract attention — literally, collect people's gazes). 情報を集める (gather information). 勇気を集める (gather courage). The word works for physical objects and abstract things alike.

Particles: 集める takes を — whatever you're collecting is your direct object. 情報を集める. You can add から for the source: みんなからお金を集める (collect money from everyone).

Potential が集められる: が — what can be collected becomes the subject. 十分なデータが集められる (enough data can be collected).

Passive に集められる: に marks who does the collecting. 先生に教室に集められた (we were gathered by the teacher into the classroom).

Causative に…を集めさせる: に marks who you make collect, を marks what they collect. 部下にデータを集めさせる (have a subordinate collect data).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 集まる (あつまる)
Dictionaryあつめるを
Negativeあつめないを
Politeあつめますを
Polite negあつめませんを
Pastあつめたを
Polite pastあつめましたを
て-formあつめてを
Volitionalあつめようを
Polite volあつめましょうを
Passiveあつめられるに
Causativeあつめさせるに／を
Caus-passあつめさせられるに
Potentialあつめられるが
Imperativeあつめろを
Imp なさいあつめなさいを
Cond ～ばあつめればを
Cond ～たらあつめたらを

声 is specifically the sound that comes out of a living throat. Dogs bark, birds sing, people talk — all 声. A car horn? That's 音 (おと, sound). The dividing line: if it came from a creature's body, it's 声. If it's mechanical or environmental, it's 音.

The word stretches into metaphor though. 国民の声 (the voice of the people — public opinion). 声を上げる (raise one's voice — speak up, protest). 声をかける (call out to someone — not literally about volume, just initiating contact). That last one is extremely common in daily Japanese: 分からないことがあったら声をかけてください (if there's anything you don't understand, just ask).

The kanji's traditional form 聲 was far more complex — it included 耳 (ear) and 殳 (a hand striking), suggesting a sound that strikes the ear. The simplified modern form 声 lost most of those pieces, but the word kept all its resonance.

初めて and 始めて are both read はじめて and both involve beginnings — but they're different words. 初めて (with the "first" kanji 初) means "for the first time" — an experience that's never happened before. 始めて (with the "start" kanji 始) is just the て-form of 始める (to begin). The one you want here is 初: the first encounter.

初 is 衤 (clothing radical) + 刀 (blade). A blade cutting cloth — the first cut into new fabric. The moment something begins for the very first time.

初めて日本に行った (I went to Japan for the first time). 初めて聞いた (first time I've heard that). The word sits naturally right before the verb it modifies. There's a common pattern worth noting: 〜て初めて — "only after doing X did I realize Y." 日本に住んで初めて分かった (I only understood after actually living in Japan). The experience had to happen before the understanding could arrive.

Three verbs read かわる exist, each with a different kanji and a different shade of change. 変わる is general transformation — something becomes different from what it was. 代わる is substitution — one thing takes the place of another. 換わる is exchange — swapping one for another. In speech they're identical. In writing, 変わる covers the vast majority of situations.

The kanji 変 is striking. The top part is a tangled mess — 亦 compressed and altered — over 夊 (slow, dragging feet). Something twisted, something off. The word carries that slightly unsettling quality: change isn't always welcome. 変な人 (a weird person) uses the same kanji. Change and strangeness are neighbors.

変わる is intransitive — change happens on its own. Its transitive partner is 変える (かえる, to change something). 天気が変わった (the weather changed) vs. 計画を変えた (I changed the plan). The weather changes by itself. The plan needs someone to change it.

Particles: 変わる takes が for the thing that changes: 景色が変わる (the scenery changes). Use に for what it changes into: 赤に変わる (turn red).

Passive に変わられる: rare, since 変わる is already intransitive. Indirect passive only — someone else's change affects you. 担当者に変わられて困った (the person in charge changed on me and it caused problems).

Causative を／に変わらせる: making something change. Uncommon — Japanese usually reaches for the transitive 変える instead. But it exists: 状況を変わらせる (make the situation change).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 変える (かえる)

Negativeかわらないが
Politeかわりますが
Polite negかわりませんが
Pastかわったが
Polite pastかわりましたが
て-formかわってが
Volitionalかわろうが
Polite volかわりましょうが

Causativeかわらせるを／に
Caus-passかわらせられるに

Imperativeかわれが
Imp なさいかわりなさいが

meaning: first (of all), to begin with, for now
lesson:
まず does something subtle: it prioritizes. Not "first" as in "the first item on a list," but "first" as in "before anything else, let's handle this." まず食べよう (let's eat first) doesn't mean eating is step one of a plan — it means eating is what matters right now, everything else can wait.

This makes まず a workhorse in conversation. Want to propose something without committing to a full plan? まずやってみよう (let's just try it first and see). Want to buy time? まず考えさせてください (let me think about it first). The word creates breathing room — it acknowledges that more steps might follow without requiring you to know what they are yet.

There's also a secondary meaning that leans toward probability: まず大丈夫だろう (it should be fine, most likely). まず間違いない (almost certainly not wrong). Here まず means "in all likelihood" — a hedged confidence. A different flavor from the "first" meaning, but both share the same instinct: setting a baseline before complications arrive.

社 (community shrine) + 会 (gathering). A community that gathers at the shrine. You've already seen both kanji in 会社 (company) — same two characters, reversed. 会社 is a specific organization. 社会 is the broader fabric: society, the social world, the system we all live in.

The word was actually coined in the Meiji era (late 1800s) when Japanese intellectuals needed a translation for the Western concept of "society." They combined these two kanji to build a word for something Japan didn't previously have a single term for — the abstract idea of a social collective beyond family and feudal structures. It's a manufactured compound, but it worked so well that it's now one of the most common nouns in Japanese.

社会人 (しゃかいじん) deserves special attention. Literally "society person," it means a working adult — someone who has graduated, entered the workforce, and become a functioning member of society. There's no clean English equivalent. Being a 社会人 carries expectations: you pay taxes, behave responsibly, know how to use keigo. 社会に出る (to enter society — start working after school) is the transition point.

A false friend — sort of. プログラム comes from English "program" but its primary meaning in Japanese is more specific: the printed booklet or leaflet you get at a concert, play, ceremony, or school event listing the schedule and performers. プログラムを見てください (please look at the program) almost always means the physical handout, not a TV show or computer software.

It can refer to the schedule or lineup of events itself: 今日のプログラム (today's program / today's schedule of events). And in computing contexts, プログラム does mean a computer program — but you'll more often hear プログラミング for the act of programming. For TV programs, Japanese reaches for 番組 (ばんぐみ) instead.

The katakana spelling is long — ten characters — making it a good drill for reading katakana at speed. Sound it out: pu-ro-gu-ra-mu. The グ (gu) catches people who expect "gra" to be one syllable. In Japanese, every consonant needs its vowel.

The kanji 力 is a pictograph of a flexed arm — specifically, the tendons and muscles of a forearm straining. In the oldest bronze inscriptions, you can see the bicep bulging. Over millennia it simplified into two clean strokes, but the physical origin is still there. This is raw bodily strength made into a symbol.

As a standalone word, ちから covers physical strength, ability, and power broadly. 力がある (to have strength / to be powerful). 力を入れる (to put in effort — literally, put in power). 全力 (ぜんりょく, full power). But in compounds, the reading shifts to りょく or りき: 能力 (のうりょく, ability), 努力 (どりょく, effort), 力士 (りきし, sumo wrestler).

力 as a radical appears in dozens of kanji, always adding a sense of force or exertion: 動 (move), 助 (help), 勉 (study — effort applied), 勝 (win). Spot the little arm in the corner of these characters and you'll see the force at work.

今 (now, this) + 回 (time, round, turn). This particular occasion, this round, this go-around. It's more specific than just "now" — it implies a series of events and you're pointing at the current one. 今回は難しかった (this time was hard) implies there were other times, and this one stood out.

The kanji 回 is a spiral — a thing going around. Its counter usage (三回, three times) is everywhere, and 今回 essentially means "the current revolution of the wheel." Pair it with its siblings: 前回 (ぜんかい, last time), 次回 (じかい, next time), 毎回 (まいかい, every time). These four show up in meetings, reviews, serialized content, games — anything that repeats.

今回 is slightly more formal than 今度 (こんど, this time / next time), and unlike 今度 it never means "next time." If you say 今回, you're always talking about now, not later.

予 (beforehand) + 定 (fix, decide). Something decided in advance. A plan, a schedule, an expected arrangement. The word lives in the space between "intention" and "commitment" — firmer than つもり (personal intention), softer than a promise.

明日の予定は？ (what are your plans for tomorrow?) is one of the most common questions in casual Japanese. 予定がある (I have plans) is the polite way to decline an invitation without specifying what those plans are. 予定通り (よていどおり, as planned / on schedule) is what you want to hear from project managers.

Key pattern: verb (dictionary form) + 予定です = "I'm planning to / I'm scheduled to." 来月引っ越す予定です (I'm planning to move next month). This is the standard way to talk about upcoming plans. It sounds natural and appropriately tentative — not a binding commitment, just what's on the calendar.

meaning: as is, still (in the current state)
lesson:
まま freezes things. Whatever state something is in, まま says it stays that way. そのまま (just like that, as it is). ドアを開けたまま出かけた (went out leaving the door open). 靴のまま入らないで (don't come in with your shoes still on).

The pattern is always the same: [state] + まま = "with that state unchanged." The state can be expressed with a past-tense verb (座ったまま, still seated), an adjective (冷たいまま, still cold), or a noun + の (裸のまま, still naked). The grammar is flexible, but the meaning is constant: nothing has changed.

There's often an undertone of something being wrong or unresolved. 分からないまま (still not understanding). 言われるまま (doing whatever you're told — passively staying in the state of being directed). まま can describe contentment (leaving things be), but just as often it describes a problem that persists because nobody addressed it.

Clipped from "television" — but only the first three syllables survived the journey into Japanese: te-re-bi. The "vision" half got dropped entirely. This kind of aggressive shortening is standard for katakana loanwords: パソコン (personal computer), リモコン (remote control), コンビニ (convenience store). Japanese borrows the word, then trims it down to something that fits comfortably in the mouth.

テレビを見る (watch TV) is one of the first full sentences most learners construct, and it's worth noting that Japanese uses 見る (see/look) rather than anything meaning "watch." You see television. You also see movies (映画を見る). The English distinction between "watch" and "see" doesn't map onto Japanese.

テレビをつける (turn on the TV) / テレビを消す (turn off the TV). テレビに出る (appear on TV) — the に marks the place where you show up. If you're talking about a specific program, that's 番組 (ばんぐみ); テレビ refers to the medium or the physical device.

減る is the intransitive half of a pair: things 減る on their own (decrease), and you 減らす (へらす) them on purpose. Population declines, savings shrink, patience wears thin — all 減る. You cut expenses, reduce staff, lower the volume — all 減らす.

The kanji 減 has water (氵) on the left and 咸 (which phonetically suggests cutting or diminishing) on the right. Water receding — like a river level dropping. お腹が減った is the classic usage: "I'm hungry" — literally, my stomach has emptied. This is the casual, slightly rough way to say it (the politer version uses 空く). If a Japanese person says 腹減った (はらへった), they're being very casual — it's a male-leaning, blunt expression of hunger.

Its opposite is 増える (ふえる, to increase). Keep the pair together: 増える / 減る, up / down. They show up side by side in news, statistics, and any conversation about trends.

Particles: 減る takes が — the thing that decreases is the subject. 人口が減る (the population decreases). 体重が減った (I lost weight).

Passive に減られる: indirect passive only — someone or something decreasing affects you negatively. スタッフに減られると困る (if the staff decreases on me, I'm in trouble).

Causative を／に減らせる: making something decrease. But Japanese typically uses the transitive 減らす instead. The causative of 減る is grammatically valid but rarely encountered.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 減らす (へらす)

Negativeへらないが
Politeへりますが
Polite negへりませんが
Pastへったが
Polite pastへりましたが
て-formへってが
Volitionalへろうが
Polite volへりましょうが

Causativeへらせるを／に
Caus-passへらせられるに
Potentialへれるが
Imperativeへれが
Imp なさいへりなさいが
Cond ～ばへればが
Cond ～たらへったらが

消える is the sound of absence. A candle goes out. A stain fades. A person vanishes from your life. The word covers every kind of disappearing — physical, visual, metaphorical — as long as the thing was there and now it's not.

The kanji 消 is water (氵) plus 肖 (to resemble/diminish). Water washing something away until it diminishes to nothing. 火が消える (the fire goes out). 電気が消えた (the lights went out). 痛みが消える (the pain disappears). 記憶が消える (memories fade away).

This is intransitive — things vanish on their own. The transitive partner is 消す (けす, to erase / turn off / extinguish), which you've already met. 消す is you killing the light. 消える is the light dying by itself. Together they form one of the cleanest intransitive/transitive pairs in the language.

Particles: 消える takes が — the thing that disappears is the subject. 音が消えた (the sound disappeared). Sometimes から marks the place it disappears from: 画面から消えた (disappeared from the screen).

Passive: rarely used, since the verb is already intransitive and describes a state rather than an action done by an agent.

Causative を消えさせる: grammatically possible but unnatural. Use 消す instead when you want the transitive meaning.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 消す (けす)

Politeきえますが
Polite negきえませんが
Pastきえたが

て-formきえてが

Imperativeきえろが

Cond ～ばきえればが
Cond ～たらきえたらが

家 (household) + 族 (tribe, clan). Your household clan. 族 is the interesting kanji here — it originally depicted a flag (方) over an arrow (矢), representing a military banner that a clan rallied under. The family as a unit marching under the same flag.

家族 is the standard, neutral word for family. Not particularly formal, not particularly casual — just the word everyone uses. 家族は何人ですか (how many people in your family?). 家族と一緒に住んでいる (I live with my family). It typically refers to the nuclear unit — the people living under your roof — though it can stretch to include extended family in context.

Japanese has several words for "family" at different registers: 家族 is the default, 家庭 (かてい) emphasizes the home environment, and 一家 (いっか) means the whole household/clan with a more dramatic ring to it. For "my family" in self-deprecating polite speech, you might hear 家のもの — but 家族 is the one you'll use 90% of the time.

The kanji 比 is two people standing side by side — literally, two figures lined up for comparison. It's one of those rare kanji where the meaning and the shape match perfectly. Two things, next to each other, being measured against one another.

AとBを比べる is the standard frame: compare A with B. 去年と比べると暖かい (compared to last year, it's warm). 兄と比べないでほしい (I don't want to be compared to my brother). The particle と marks what you're comparing against, while を marks the things being compared.

An extremely common set phrase: 〜に比べて — "compared to." 日本に比べて物価が安い (compared to Japan, prices are cheap). This て-form usage shows up constantly in conversation and writing. It's one of those constructions where the grammar is technically a verb but it functions more like a preposition.

Particles: 比べる takes を for the things being compared: 二つの案を比べる. Use と to mark the benchmark: 前回と比べる. In the に比べて pattern, に marks the reference point.

Potential が比べられる: が — what can be compared becomes the subject. 簡単に比べられる (can be easily compared).

Passive に比べられる: に marks who does the comparing — or what you're compared to. いつも姉に比べられる (I'm always compared to my older sister).

Causative に…を比べさせる: に marks who you have compare, を marks what they compare. 学生に二つの文を比べさせる (have students compare two sentences).

meaning: to be born, come into existence
lesson:
生 is one of the most versatile kanji in the language. It has at least ten different readings and appears in hundreds of compounds. As う（まれる） it means "to be born"; as い（きる）, "to live"; as なま, "raw"; as せい, "life" in compounds. All orbiting the same core idea: alive, growing, fresh.

生まれる is intransitive — you are born; nobody "births" you in this construction. (The transitive partner 生む (うむ, to give birth) takes the mother as the subject.) 東京で生まれた (I was born in Tokyo). 新しいアイデアが生まれた (a new idea was born). The word extends naturally to anything that comes into existence — ideas, trends, technologies, works of art.

A useful conversational pattern: 生まれも育ちも大阪 (born and raised in Osaka). 生まれ (the noun form, "birth") pairs neatly with 育ち (upbringing) to locate someone's whole origin story in one phrase.

Particles: 生まれる takes が for what's born: 子供が生まれた. Use で for where: 大阪で生まれた. Use に for the time or family: 1990年に生まれた.

Passive: Already intransitive and semi-passive in meaning ("to be born"), so the passive form is essentially unused.

Causative を生まれさせる: unnatural. Use 生む (to give birth to) or 生み出す (to create/produce) for the active meaning.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 生む (うむ)

Negativeうまれないが
Politeうまれますが
Polite negうまれませんが

て-formうまれてが

Caus-passうまれさせられるに

Imperativeうまれろが
Imp なさいうまれなさいが
Cond ～ばうまれればが
Cond ～たらうまれたらが

Something for nothing. ただでもらった (I got it for free). 入場はただです (admission is free). The word is blunt and colloquial — the kind of thing you'd say to a friend, not write in a formal announcement. For that, you'd use 無料 (むりょう).

But ただ has a second, completely unrelated meaning that shows up even more often: "just, merely, only." ただの友達 (just a friend — nothing more). ただし (however — a formal conjunction). These aren't the same word historically, but they've collapsed into the same sound. Context separates them instantly: if money is involved, it means free. Otherwise, it means "merely."

There's a proverb: ただより高いものはない (nothing is more expensive than free). The Japanese instinct that free things come with hidden costs — social obligations, implied debts, strings attached. It's not cynicism so much as awareness of the unspoken exchange beneath every gift.

これ + ら = the plural. You know これ (this); ら is a suffix that loosely pluralizes. これらの問題 (these problems). これらは全部私のです (these are all mine).

Japanese doesn't normally mark plurals, though. これ alone can already mean "this" or "these" depending on context. So when does これら earn its place? When you need to be explicitly clear that you're talking about multiple items, especially in writing. これら is more bookish than conversational — you'll see it in reports, academic writing, and formal speech more than in casual talk. In conversation, most people just say これ and gesture at the whole group.

The same ら suffix shows up in 彼ら (かれら, they) and 私たち (where たち serves the same pluralizing function). But ら and たち aren't quite interchangeable: たち is warmer, more inclusive. ら is cooler, more clinical. You'd never say これたち.

meaning: to investigate, examine, check, look into
lesson:
調べる is the verb for active information-seeking. Not casual browsing — deliberate checking. 辞書で調べる (look it up in a dictionary). ネットで調べる (look it up online). 警察が事件を調べている (the police are investigating the case). It scales from "let me Google that" to "a team of detectives is on it."

The kanji 調 has 言 (words) on the left and 周 (around, thorough) on the right. Words going around thoroughly — examining from all sides. In compounds, the reading is usually ちょう: 調査 (ちょうさ, investigation/survey), 調整 (ちょうせい, adjustment).

調べる vs 探す: Both involve looking for something, but they're different searches. 探す (さがす) is searching for a physical thing or person — lost keys, a specific restaurant, a missing child. 調べる is searching for information or examining something for understanding. You 探す your phone. You 調べる why it stopped working.

Particles: 調べる takes を — the thing you're investigating is the direct object. 原因を調べる (investigate the cause). Use で for the tool or method: ネットで調べる.

Potential が調べられる: が — what can be investigated becomes the subject. スマホで簡単に調べられる (you can easily look it up on your phone).

Passive に調べられる: に marks who investigates. 警察に調べられた (I was investigated by the police).

Causative に…を調べさせる: に marks who you have investigate, を marks what. 部下にデータを調べさせた (I had my subordinate check the data).

一段 Ichidan他動詞

Caus-passしらべさせられるに

Cond ～たらしらべたらを

事 (thing, matter) + 故 (reason, cause, old). A "matter with a cause" — something that happened, and not for good reasons. The word leans negative: car crashes, workplace incidents, nuclear disasters. 交通事故 (こうつうじこ, traffic accident) is the compound you'll hear most.

故 is a kanji worth knowing on its own. It means "old" or "the past" in some contexts (故郷, こきょう, hometown) and "reason/cause" in others (故に, ゆえに, therefore — very literary). In 事故, it brings the sense of causation gone wrong — an event with an unfortunate cause.

事故に遭う (じこにあう, to meet with an accident) uses 遭う, the "encounter" verb reserved for bad luck. You don't 遭う good fortune — the verb itself tells you the encounter was unwelcome. 事故を起こす (to cause an accident) is the other key pattern — 起こす (to cause) puts the blame on the subject.

電 (electricity) + 話 (talk). Electric talk. The compound was coined when the telephone first arrived in Japan in the 1870s, and it's one of those translations that nails it: speech sent through electricity. Chinese, Korean, and Vietnamese all adopted versions of the same compound.

電話 is a する-noun — slap する on it and it becomes a verb: 電話する (to phone someone). 電話をかける (to make a call — literally, hang/cast a call) is the slightly more specific phrasing. 電話に出る (to answer the phone — "come out to" the phone). 電話を切る (to hang up — "cut" the call).

In the smartphone age, 電話 still specifically means a voice call. Texting is メール or メッセージ. So 電話しようか (shall I call you?) is explicitly proposing voice over text — sometimes the more intimate or urgent choice. The word 携帯 (けいたい) or スマホ refers to the device; 電話 refers to the act of calling or the concept of telephony.

外 (outside) + 国 (country). An outside country. The word is simple, but the worldview it encodes isn't: Japan is the default, and everything else is 外 — outside. There's no neutral "other country" in daily Japanese. You're either in Japan or you're 外 of it.

This inside/outside framing runs deep. 外国人 (がいこくじん, foreigner) is the standard, neutral term for a non-Japanese person. 外国語 (がいこくご, foreign language). 外国に行く (go abroad). The word isn't rude — it's the default, polite term. But it does reveal how Japanese naturally categorizes: 内 (inside, us) versus 外 (outside, them).

外国 vs 海外: Both mean "abroad" but with different textures. 外国 emphasizes the country (a specific foreign nation or foreign nations in general). 海外 (かいがい, literally "across the sea") emphasizes the act of being overseas — travel, experience, distance. 外国の文化 (foreign culture) vs. 海外に住む (live overseas).

銀 (silver) + 行 (firm, business, row). A silver firm. You met 行 in lesson 1 as いく (to go) — but here it's in its compound reading こう, carrying the older meaning of "a row of shops" or "a business establishment." The "silver" comes from the historical role of silver as currency in East Asian trade. Chinese banks were literally places that dealt in silver.

Modern 銀行 is just a bank — ATMs, savings accounts, mortgages. 銀行に行く (go to the bank). 銀行に預ける (deposit at the bank). 銀行から下ろす (withdraw from the bank). Japan is still more cash-oriented than many countries, so 銀行 and ATM come up in daily life frequently.

The kanji 銀 has the metal radical 金 on the left (which itself means gold — all metals get the gold radical) and 艮 on the right, which contributes the sound. 銀 appears in 銀色 (ぎんいろ, silver color) and the famous 銀座 (ぎんざ, literally "silver seat" — the Tokyo shopping district, named after a historical silver mint).
